sentences.text: Hi, Janae.
Hello, how are you?
Good, how are you doing?
Thanks.
I haven't had a chance to get on any of the demo calls yet, but I have watched the videos of the demo calls.
So…
That's weird.
The last one was an hour and a half long, so I made a commitment.
Well, I mean, there were… you know, I did fast-forward through some of it.
We need to… we need to trim some of the fat, we're talking too much.
Yeah.
of the meeting, so… which I was able to do on my own, so I was able to walk through that, so…
Great, well, what jumped out to you?
You know what?
been enough in the tool, the current tool, that's very similar.
Yeah.
The applications are very similar, so it's pretty easy to navigate, primarily because I've been in the tool, too.

But I did like the data that was feeding in, so I kind of compared what we're currently looking at from just… you know, there's some pricing recommendations already built into the current tool, but then compared that on the… Just through the video, what that actually looks like when you're actually in a pricing tool dedicated for that.
And you can see with the information that comes in, it's providing a much better outcome.

Not that we're really using the pricing recommendation in your current tool, but it's a good way to… feel like it's more of just kind of looking at the here's the pricing, you know, we probably think you fit in here, where there's a little bit more property data that feeds in, you know, into the actual pricing tool.
So…

Yeah, yeah, it's interesting in the sense that we've, over the last 4 years, seen about 16 million units of lease data, and so that amount of data informing an algorithm versus incumbent price optimization software is a fairly interesting component, so…
Great.
I knew there was some… So, we've been working on our own pricing tool, you know.
When the industry saw a little shift in, you know, this whole idea of.

Yield Star and sharing back-end data, you know, we decided at that point that you know, we don't want to be at risk of being part of anything or having exposure on that, so how can we take our own data, our own individual data, and how can we build that out?
And we worked with a group from, University of Utah, which we're here to.
Yeah.
hired a bunch of data scientists to come in and kind of use it as part of their capstone project, and on, and beyond.
And so, you know, I think we have a decent tool, but it's… we don't have enough time to dedicate the resources to.
Brilliant.
Where we… where we need to put it.
How did you go about piloting that internally?
Was there a select group of properties, same way that you…
highlighted it yet.
We worked with Liz, our pricing manager, to… Okay.
Here's where she would price it, here's what the tool is saying, and it really wasn't that far off.
It's the ease and simplicity of it.
It's a challenge, and that's just going to take more development time if we were going to commit to it, to go in and to… it's pretty…
data-heavy right now, so it looks very data-heavy.
I mean, it's still kind of a test box that came out of, you know, working with a bunch of data scientists.
And so, now you need the input from practicality or user interface is really what you need on the… what we need to put on it.
And… we just don't… we think it's a while out before we'll get there.
So, if we… if we're going to pursue this avenue with you, we'll abandon, you know, that application.
So, that's where we're at, is a little bit of a crossroads.
We feel like there's a little bit of urgency on our part, but we.
Like.
but take the time and build ours out.
We've been doing that.
not currently using, but doing that, and just kind of testing some information, about 8 months now.
And so… but where we're not really ready to take it out to market to our own group, that's where I would say, you know, let's look at this partnership in more detail, and see if it's something.
Now, I know we're discussing pricing.
But we haven't really tested it either, so I think we may bring the cart before the horse just a little bit as this conversation.
Yep.
Great for me to be involved with it.

And we can talk about pricing and maybe get there, but we… I mean, I do want to test the tool to see if this is where we can roll it out to our entire portfolio, and that's what we would do if we're comfortable, you know, out in the field with how the recommendations are coming in and see if we can make some impact.
Yeah, it sounds… I mean, it sounds like we're at a good place from a timing perspective.
I know that Liz is pretty excited about the tool, and it feels like, oh, we didn't get direct feedback, that it was received well by the VPs that roll up to you as well.
Great.
I think from a pricing perspective, we're always going to be willing to work with you there.

The main purpose for the call today with Jamie was just there's a lot of moving components in terms of… product offerings with DepartmentIQ, so I wanted to understand where the interests were, so that we can get to best rates with you all.
But I did introduce Liz to, Brittany Schevel, who was at RealPage for a very long time.
She's a new director of pricing on our team, who's running the pilot process.
And so, from my understanding, where we're at right now is just waiting for the, properties that we'd actually want to pilot, and then we can get that… start to get that set up for you all.
Great.
How long does that take to get those set up?
Generally, like, a week to two weeks.
It's a back-and-forth process to make sure that we're really QAing and getting the recommendations, keyed in.
And then, for the purpose of, pilot process, we typically have a few factors that we Ask for in terms of the assets that we're working with.
Nothing, nothing too intense, just certain, unit sizes so that we can calibrate the recommendations.
A certain amount of history, like, i.
e, no major property management software, where a lot of at least history was, was lost.
Huh.
Once we have that, it's a little bit of work on our end, and some calibration, then we're good to go.
So when you're saying some data, when we give you the back-end data or API access to our Yardi platform, are you saying we need to give you access to a certain data point?
So you're gonna put… there's kind of two methodologies of this, is you can push your actual rent roll into the platform, or you can use the ApartmentIQ public data.
So there's, like, a public-only mode.
For particular markets that you're able to toggle on.
But it would be a one-way integration at this point, and then you'd be receiving the recommendations directly out of the software for the purpose of the pilot.
So you would not be using our data to make any recommendations.
We would, if you'd like to go that route.
We can also use the… we can also use only what's publicly listed, so I don't know if those two things are different, if you're hiding units, or, etc, but there's two optionalities with that.
We are hiding units, and that's… That is really interesting, because I found the data in your guys' software shows our full availability, but we hide units, which most.
Yeah.
I've actually been asking Liz about that for a while.
How does that… how does… how are you getting our full data when we're not providing our full data?
about 5 years of learning how to, learning how to scrape, essentially.
So, certain things like ingrain or sitemap, there's certain things that are listed publicly online that aren't, like, marketing-facing or wouldn't be showing on the property website.
So that's how we go about, doing that.
So it's like a series of toggling that we're running in the different URLs that we're visiting to get those units off.
Okay?
Yeah.
So when you start using our…
back in… If you're using our own data to set pricing, which we can do.
What difference does that make in the overall performance or the outcomes that are recommended?
Do you find that using individual property data gives a stronger recommendation, or do you feel like it's on par with just the public scrape?
We've tested both.
Both are strong.

I think the phase that we're at as a product is we've got about 100,000 units that are under pilot right now, and so I wouldn't want to give you an answer until we get through that process and are able to confirm with our partners what they feel the stronger recommendations were.
Okay, so that's just in pilot right now.
Who's that… who are you with, or who are you using for your test groups?
I'm not sure that I'm at liberty to say it.
I will say that, the tool, and I think maybe you got this background from the calls we were on, but the tool was developed in coincidence with some larger enterprise clients of ours.
So, I think top 10, top 50 NMHC.
Operators, yeah.
Okay.
Okay.
Okay, so that gives me some context how long it would take to… do a test group, and I'll make sure Liz gets that information over to Brittany on the sites that we would like to run a test on.
Now, as far as… When you say there's products.
I mean, we're looking at the pricing tool.
I think you have a reporting Or, like, an MPF report, similar to a Yield Star report application, correct?
Yeah, so all your typical revenue management software reports will be in there, and then there is the optionality, right?
So there's, like, the product as is, and then there is the advisor cost, if you'd like to add that on.
Which, that's where we have our own advisor, so we… that's where we would not need that.

We have our own, you know, pricing manager, our own advisor who's been doing this now since You know, basically, yield management software has come out, so she's been in this role for 10 plus years, and feels pretty good about that, so… So that is something that we would keep in-house.
That's great.
I think one of the early pieces of feedback that we've gotten is that we're not going to knock you for not accepting recommendations.
This is kind of the standard in the space, and we're not going to require that you have an advisor as well.
Great.
Well, we've always pushed back that without partnerships in the past, too.
We actually have a…
We're probably one of the lowest adoption rates, which is good and bad.
We were probably around 60-70% adoption rate of rates over the course of, you know, our history with Yield Star, so…
It's not too bad at all.
So, yeah.
Yeah, in terms of the product lines, the main kind of component that I wanted to touch on is, functionally, we've introduced a competitor to CoStar.
So, broader market rented performance, in addition to, taking the apartment IQ data, which is updating that 35 million units.
And layering in, new supply data, demographic information.
And then most recently, we partnered with Real Capital Analytics, if you're familiar with them at all.
So, that is shoring up things like ownership and transactions information.
And there's really 3 kind of modules within this, what we call, like, the tool will be called Explore, or something to that effect.
We're not… we haven't ironed out the branding yet, but…
Hmm.
You have that market performance, and you can go market down to sub-market and select boundaries, etc.
It operates much like a co-star.
There is a forecasting module within that as well.
So, around 60 days ago, he's newer, we brought over Mike Taylor, actually, from, from CoStar.
He had a little gap in there for an NDA, but… We armed him with our data and our, modeling, and a forecasting module just went live.
It was, like, a week or two ago.
So it's gonna forecast out 5 years of rents, so pretty standard there.
Okay.
The third component, which is, and that's your Explore model, right?
It is, yep.
I've taken some notes.
Sometimes people think I'm on my phone.
I'm not on my phone, I'm taking notes.
Yeah, yeah.
Okay?
And then there's a market comparison, tool within there as well.
So, the ability across, I believe the three geographies we have in there right now is MSA, county, and city.
Okay.
So, the ability to track, performance, supply, transactions, and demographics.
Over any, any trend timeframe that you would want to look at.
To help guide, next opportunities.
So you can probably gather, but effectively, we're trying to move further up the funnel.
IQ is often used in underwriting support on the rented performance, so we want to start to provide some of that other information alongside of that.
Yes.
So, in the pricing offering.
that you guys provided us, that was just for apartment, for the… the… basically for the rent, apartment IQ.
software correctly.
Yeah, so I think the way that we thought about this was, we knew it would be between a dollar and two dollars a unit.
I think on that first call, it was said, get us closer to a dollar.
We said, okay, we won't argue that.
And so that was… the dollar was specific to the revenue management software.

And then I think Jamie's counter ask was that, if we do choose to move forward with the revenue management software, it would be in dollar inclusive of the market survey, so that the revenue management tool would drop to, effectively, 85 cents a year.
death.
And the market survey would stay where it's at, which is… One of the more incredible grandfather rates I've seen, I think at, like, 16 cents a unit.
We got in early, Lonnie.
It's like a tenth of market, but it's… we're all good with it.
You guys were just starting out.
Yeah.
I had reached out and said, hey, let's… this was probably… I mean, how old's the company?
Probably 10, 15 years?
October 2020.
Oh, the hot tub workspace?
Oh.
Yeah.
For some reason, I think it's older than that.
Well, Rentable, the parent company, it's become pretty interesting.
We started as an ILS, and we scaled that business to, like… I've been here the whole time, so I've got a I've seen it all.
We scaled that to, like, a $15 million business.
We raised.
With Susquehanna Capital out of Philadelphia.
And, like, as soon as that money hit our bank, COVID happened, and a thousand advertising customers we had wanted to know.
What data we had on how markets were operating.
So we created that, and used a lot of artificial intelligence, and now we actually have a Google Business product, Maven, that is, automating,
copy and content across Google, across social now, there's a chat function to it.
So there's functionally, like, 3 parts of our business now.
Huh.
as a whole has been around 15 years, IQ for about 5.
Okay, that makes more… maybe more sense.
Yeah.
That hits the timeline maybe just a little bit more than what I was.
Yep.
thinking, so…
Yeah, so the main… the, and I think the other component that was in play here was a data sync.
I think we were feeding you all data.
I'm wondering if that was for that revenue management software you were creating, or if that's going elsewhere.
That is for an internal, and we wouldn't need that data sync anymore.
Right.
So, that is not something we would need moving forward from this.
And so, I believe we're paying… How much are we paying?
7 cents a unit or something to that effect, yeah.
Yeah, so we would no longer need that.
Totally good on our end.
So the main kind of outstanding component with Jamie, just so you all have all the information that you need as we hopefully go into this pilot, was that Explore side of things.
And there's no real rush on that.
That is priced a bit differently because it's license-based versus our typical per-unit pricing that we're doing.
Yeah, and I think moving forward today, we'll move forward without that Explorer, but if you don't mind kind of throwing out what pricing generally looks like for that model.
Yeah, so, up to 5 users is $15,000 annually.
Okay.
Up to 10 users is $25,000 annually, and then up to 25 is $50,000 annually.
Okay.
I have heard whispers that might go to, like, unit brackets, so I, if you have 20,000 units, it's this cost.
Temporary versus users.
Yeah, that's not confirmed yet.
So, as of today, that's what the pricing is, is unit-based, unit-based.
Okay.
those brackets.
All right.
Well, I think for what we need…
to make the decision is, I think we're good with the pricing, with the market survey, plus the tool.
At the dollar a unit.
We're moving the, data sync, and then just getting the pilot going now.
If the pilot doesn't go well.
Yep.
And we just…
105% renewal, I would guess, something like that.
Okay.
Yeah.
I don't know, I don't dabble in that much.
I can confirm with the.
the RCS team on that, but I think they're… they asked between 5% and 7%.
Okay.
How that's gonna go great, though.
Well, I'm hoping, and we're looking forward to it.
I mean, we don't anticipate things will go bad, but we've done pilots before and realized it doesn't work with it.
Yes.
You know, that there are some hiccups, that's why everyone pilots it, right?
Yes, and I would encourage as many properties as you all want to get on the platform, I would be happy to do it.
So I've sent those factors over as to essentially what we're looking for to make sure that both parties are set up for success.
Right.
But we're happy with Liz, right?
Yes.
Right.
Yeah, so I saw Brittany followed up with her today, so… I'll give it a day or two, and then check back in there.
Okay, great.
And I'll follow up with her as well, so…
I think that answers all the questions I had.
I know Kaino was kind of listening in.
Kaino is our CMO, and works closely with, you know, Liz and our, you know, revenue management department as well.
And has been working on, kind of, our internal program, and so, Kainoa's on the call, and so I don't know if you have any questions, additional ones?
Nope, I was just gonna make sure we were good to remove that data sync, so looks like we're good on that.

Yeah, all good on that, and then, kind of, I'm sure you'll be working with, with Brittany as this, picks up, so… She has about 15 years of, revenue management history, and… I wouldn't try and answer any questions you asked today without consulting with her first, so…
Ready?
Awesome.
All right, great.
Well, I'll talk to Liz, we'll give you those lists of properties then, and we'll, and then just work with Jamie on the contract, and she will get that to me for execution.
Right.
So, in terms of contracting, re-signature of the market survey now, removal of the data sync…
Yes.
Contracting revenue management would occur following a successful pilot.
on the pilot, yep.
Perfect.
Okay, I'll work with Jamie on that, and appreciate your time.
Alright, thank you, appreciate it.
Wonderful city, Mark.
Right.
